Second-highest grasp score (0.7001); identical geometry to node-8, also a pin; may be assembled similarly after or alongside node-8.

Attach Dovetail Clip (node-2) onto base via planar contact

content.text: Generated evidence bundle with 12 image artifacts.

relation_hypotheses.reason: Dovetail Clip (node-2) appears to be mounted on the flange of the main SOLID part (node-10), with its purple feature aligned to a hex socket in the SOLID; the dovetail geometry suggests a sliding fit into a matching groove on node-10.

Screw (node-3) is positioned coaxially with one of the four peripheral holes in the flange view (first image), indicating it fastens through node-10; similar for nodes 4–7, all likely threaded into node-10.

The 1/8" pin (node-8) is small and cylindrical; in the third image, a small cylindrical feature near the clip’s interface suggests a pin may secure rotational freedom or align the clip—consistent with typical dovetail retention.

subassemblies.reason: Two pins (node-8, node-9) and the Dovetail Clip (node-2) form a functional subassembly: the clip likely slides onto the main body and is retained/aligned by the two pins, as suggested by symmetry and relative positioning in the 3D views. No direct visual evidence of pins inserted, but their count and size match typical dual-pin alignment schemes.

Five identical screws are symmetrically placed around the flange (first image shows 4 visible, one likely hidden behind); they collectively form a fastening subassembly securing the flange to another component (likely node-10 itself or an unseen mating part). Given only 9 parts listed and node-10 being the only large structural part, they fasten to node-10. The fifth screw (node-7) may be on the opposite side or obscured.

base_part.reason: SOLID (node-10) is the largest, structurally dominant part with a central bore and peripheral holes; all other parts either attach to it (screws, clip) or are small fasteners. Its geometry (flange + hub) matches typical base components in mechanical assemblies.

grasp_plan.reason: The top flange surface is flat and large, suitable for vacuum suction; the central bore allows internal gripping or centering. Avoiding screw holes prevents damage; this provides stable, non-slip grasp for orientation during assembly.

The dovetail clip has a distinct slot and a protruding purple feature; gripping the flat bottom of the slot and the side of the purple feature provides stable, form-fit grasp without obscuring mating surfaces. Caution needed due to small size.
Cylindrical shank and flat head make collet ideal for precise axial alignment; avoids slippage during insertion. Same applies to nodes 4–7.

assembly_sequence.reason: Clip must be installed before screws to avoid interference; the dovetail likely slides radially onto node-10’s hub before being pinned or clamped.
One pin (node-8) may be inserted after clip placement to lock it axially; however, visual evidence is indirect—this step is tentative.
Second pin (node-9) likely follows for symmetry and redundancy; low confidence due to lack of explicit visual confirmation of pin holes in clip.
Screws are typically installed last to clamp the assembly; node-3 is one of five identical screws, installed in sequence after clip/pin subassembly is seated.
Second screw, following standard torque-pattern (e.g., cross pattern) to ensure even clamping.
Fifth screw—likely on the backside or obscured in current views; included based on part list symmetry and typical over-constraint for rigidity.

uncertainties: Pin (node-8, node-9) insertion points not visually confirmed; may be press-fit, threaded, or absent (clip may be self-locking).
Exact mating interface between node-2 and node-10 (e.g., groove depth, interference) cannot be determined from render alone.
Whether the five screws all engage directly with node-10 or some pass through to a secondary part (not listed) is ambiguous; assumed direct based on part count and geometry.
The purple feature on node-2 may be a separate insert (not modeled as separate part), affecting subassembly composition.

relation_hypotheses.reason: Visual evidence from local overlay and face masks confirms a true pin-in-hole insertion: the coaxial cylinder pair (mesh-7:face-1 & mesh-8:face-36) shows near-zero gap (0.0002) and perfect alignment, while the planar faces (mesh-7:face-3/4 & mesh-8:face-35/38) are in contact with the pin fully seated, resolving the initial ambiguity about proximity vs. assembly.

subassemblies.reason: The confirmed pin-in-hole relation forms a canonical subassembly. The visual evidence shows the pin is fully inserted and constrained by both the cylindrical bore and the planar shoulder of the base part (node-10), making this a stable, self-contained subassembly.

base_part.reason: node-10 has the highest base candidate score (1.0) due to its large volume, central spatial position, and high connectivity. All other parts (pins, screws, clip) are designed to attach to it, confirming it as the structural foundation.

grasp_plan.reason: The planar grip pair on the small cylindrical pin (node-8) offers a robust, accessible grasp point. Its high score and simple geometry make it ideal for an early assembly step, consistent with the assembly sequence.
Identical to node-8, this pin has a high-scoring planar grip pair. It will be grasped and inserted into node-10 immediately after or alongside node-8, as part of the same subassembly operation.
The Dovetail Clip (node-2) has a large planar contact surface with the base. Grasping its top flange (faces 12/13) provides a stable approach for the subsequent planar mating step.

assembly_sequence.reason: Start with the base (node-10). The first moving part is the simplest pin (node-8), which can be easily grasped and inserted into its corresponding hole with minimal interference.
Insert the second pin (node-9) into its hole in the base. This step is identical to step 1 and can be performed concurrently or sequentially, forming the confirmed subassembly.
After the pins are seated, attach the Dovetail Clip (node-2) onto the base using its planar contact surfaces. The clip likely slides over the pins, so they must be in place first.
Install the first screw (node-3) into its threaded hole. Screws are typically installed after primary structural elements (pins, clips) are in place to avoid interference.
Install the second screw (node-4). The remaining screws (node-5, node-6, node-7) follow in a similar pattern, completing the fastening sequence.

uncertainties: The exact order of screw installation (node-3 to node-7) is not strongly differentiated by the evidence; they are all high-confidence insertable-coaxial candidates with similar scores and geometries.
While the pin-in-hole relation is confirmed, the precise functional role of the pins (e.g., alignment only vs. load-bearing) cannot be determined from geometry alone.
